romania.title: „A intrat în tufe și nu știu ce s-a putut întâmpla”. Mărturia tatălui copilului căutat cu elicopterul și scafandrii în Sibiu
romania.summary: Tatăl copilului de 5 ani dispărut luni în județul Sibiu a povestit cum, într-un moment de neatenție, băiatul s-a îndepărtat de el într-o zonă împădurită de la marginea localității Sebeșu de Jos, unde bărbatul repara un gard electric. La aproape 24 de ore de la dispariție, autoritățile continuă căutările de amploare cu sute de salvatori și voluntari, echipe de scafandri și un elicopter al IGAV, în condițiile în care zona este cunoscută de localnici pentru prezența animalelor sălbatice.

romania.title: Fost comandant al unităţii de paraşutişti antitero de la SRI, „transferat” de Pro TV. Ce rol va avea
romania.summary: Postul de televiziune Pro TV a anunțat că de acum înainte va avea o nouă poziţie: cea de Head of Security, care va fi ocupată de Cătălin Netcu, scrie marți Pagina de Media. Cătălin Netcu a condus, în trecut, o unitate SRI și, în noua poziție, „va coordona strategia de securitate a companiei, cu responsabilităţi …

Argumentele aduse de aceștia sunt considerate de-a dreptul ridicole. „Cum poți să spui că Domnul Georgescu nu avea acei bani, cât de ticălos să fii să susții așa ceva?”, a […] The post Fanii lui Georgescu știu de unde are acesta milioane pentru donații: din salariul de la Real Madrid appeared first on Times New Roman .

În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

romania.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

romania.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
romania.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

medical.title: Tiny worms, with help from researchers, may hold key to treating rare childhood disease
medical.summary: A new worm model developed by Brown University researchers could play a key role in treating a rare genetic disease that causes paralysis in children and worsens with age. Developed in the lab of neuroscientist Anne Hart, a genetically engineered C. elegans nematode model provides a fast, inexpensive way to evaluate potential drug treatments for alternating hemiplegia of childhood, or AHC, a disorder that currently has no cure or effective treatments.

medical.title: Research into Friedreich's ataxia reveals how DNA folding can silence a key gene
medical.summary: Researchers have uncovered a fundamental rule that governs how genes are physically arranged inside the cell nucleus, and how disruptions to that organization can contribute to human disease.

medical.title: AI chatbot shows promise in combating health misinformation
medical.summary: Can AI help people resist misinformation? Initial research findings suggest that artificial intelligence-driven conversations can strengthen people's resilience to health misinformation, outperforming traditional educational methods.

medical.title: Heat-related hospitalizations rose over two decades. Black adults, low-income communities disproportionately affected
medical.summary: A study examined racial and ethnic disparities in rates of heat-related illness (HRI) hospitalizations among U.S. adults from 1998 to 2022. The findings showed that hospitalization rates for HRIs have increased overall in the U.S., with Black adults experiencing the highest rates and greatest increases. People residing in low-income ZIP codes were also disproportionately affected. The findings are published in the Annals of Internal Medicine.

medical.title: Food allergy researchers predict oral food challenge tests as an obstacle to future food allergy trials
medical.summary: A new international perspective led by UNC School of Medicine researchers highlights that oral food challenges, historically considered essential to food allergy clinical trials, are now limiting who can participate and threatening to slow progress in food allergy research.

medical.title: Ivermectin prescriptions more than double after celebrity endorses it as a cancer treatment on a high-profile podcast
medical.summary: Prescriptions for the anti-parasite medication ivermectin doubled in the months following an endorsement by a celebrity during a high-profile podcast as an off-label cancer treatment, a UCLA-led study finds.

medical.title: More than two million Australian middle-aged men are drinking at risky levels
medical.summary: The first national in-depth study to examine alcohol use among Australian middle-aged men has found that more than 2 million are drinking at levels that put their health at risk of harm. The new Flinders University research, in partnership with the Alcohol and Drug Foundation (ADF), analyzed national data from the 2019 and 2022/23 National Drug Strategy Household Survey to identify factors linked to risky drinking among midlife men aged 30–59, comparing the younger (30–44) and older (45–59) age groups.

medical.title: Study suggests immunotherapy may strengthen treatment for aggressive prostate cancer
medical.summary: Researchers at Moffitt Cancer Center report encouraging early results from a phase 2 study examining whether immunotherapy can improve results when added to a radiotherapy-based treatment regimen for men with aggressive prostate cancer. The work, led by Kosj Yamoah, MD, Ph.D., was published in the Journal for ImmunoTherapy of Cancer.

medical.title: Are your meds and DNA a bad match? This test alerts clinicians
medical.summary: Differences in people's genes can affect how they respond to medications, including how well a drug works and the chances of side effects. About 1 in 10 of prescriptions given to patients outside the hospital don't work as well as they should because of differences in people's genes, according to a review of U.S. studies by UC San Francisco health economics professor Kathryn Phillips, Ph.D., and colleagues.

medical.title: A two-way street: The overlapping world of premenstrual disorders and mental health conditions
medical.summary: Premenstrual disorders and psychiatric conditions often seem to flock together, and researchers now have data to make a case for it. A large Swedish study involving over 3 million women revealed a striking two-way path between premenstrual disorders (PMDs)—premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD) and premenstrual syndrome (PMS)—and common mental health conditions like depression and anxiety.

medical.title: Boosting one protein helps the brain fight Alzheimer’s
medical.summary: Scientists have discovered a way to help the brain clean itself of harmful Alzheimer’s plaques by activating its own support cells. By increasing a protein called Sox9, researchers were able to boost the activity of astrocytes, star shaped cells that help maintain brain health. In mice that already showed memory problems, this approach reduced plaque buildup and preserved cognitive function over time.

medical.title: Scientists discover a hidden brain “cleaning” effect triggered by movement
medical.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising link between simple body movement and brain health: every time you tighten your abdominal muscles—even slightly—your brain may gently sway inside your skull. This subtle motion, triggered by pressure changes in connected blood vessels, appears to help circulate cerebrospinal fluid around the brain, potentially flushing out harmful waste.

medical.title: Pesticide exposure linked to 150% higher cancer risk in major study
medical.summary: A major new study finds that living in pesticide-heavy environments could raise cancer risk by up to 150%, even when the chemicals are considered “safe” on their own. The research suggests these mixtures may silently damage cells years before cancer appears.

science.title: One drug, two cleanup crews: A built-in backup for targeted protein degradation
science.summary: Most drugs work by inhibition: they block a protein's activity but leave the protein itself intact. Targeted protein degradation takes a fundamentally different approach, harnessing the cell's own quality-control machinery to remove proteins entirely.

science.title: We found hundreds of huge ancient mass graves hidden in the Sahara desert
science.summary: We have been on a years-long campaign of satellite remote sensing of the vast desert landscapes in Eastern Sudan.

science.title: Birds can suffer serious harm from heat waves
science.summary: Extreme weather poses a big threat to birds. Yet there is a lack of both knowledge and methods for measuring its negative effects. In a new study published in Trends in Ecology & Evolution, researchers from Lund University in Sweden have identified knowledge gaps and propose practical tools for analyzing the risks to birds.

science.title: Scientists use AI to interpret the sun's acoustic heartbeat
science.summary: A new AI-based approach that can "hear" inside the sun could give vital signs of the solar disturbances that have significant effects in near-Earth space and on human activities. The solar cycle is an approximate 11-year period during which the sun's magnetic activity rises and falls. The cycle begins relatively calmly. However, as it progresses, the magnetic field becomes more aggressive, leading to a surge in sunspot numbers, solar flares, and coronal mass ejections. These eruptions can disrupt satellites and power grids and thus have a direct impact on our communication technology.

science.title: Engineered exosomes reverse sleep deprivation brain damage in mice
science.summary: Sleep is a vital physiological process that allows humans and other animals to restore both the mind and body, while also consolidating memories, clearing out toxins and regulating their metabolism. Several past studies showed that getting insufficient sleep for prolonged periods of time can trigger inflammatory responses and can negatively impact people's memory, mood, attention and decision-making.

science.title: Hello Universe: NASA’s Next-Gen Space Processor Undergoes Testing
science.summary: NASA’s High Performance Spaceflight Computing project aims to dramatically improve the computing power of spacecraft. Missions need processors that can withstand the harsh space environment, so they use chips developed years ago that are hardy and reliable. But upgraded chips are needed to enable the development of autonomous spacecraft, accelerate the rate of scientific discovery […]

science.title: New quantum protocol breaks distance and speed barriers in fiber networks
science.summary: Scientists at the University of Science and Technology of China have successfully deployed a multi-mode quantum relay network, achieving matter–matter entanglement over 14.5 kilometers, according to media reports.

science.title: I Am Artemis: Kathleen Harmon
science.summary: Listen to this audio excerpt from Kathleen Harmon, the Artemis II Mission Interface Manager for NASA’s Deep Space Network: Captivated by Apollo launches on her television as a child, Kathleen Harmon now plays a key role in NASA’s Artemis program. Harmon serves as the Artemis II mission interface manager for NASA’s Deep Space Network, an […]

science.title: 'Implosion carving' shrinks 3D photonic devices 2,000-fold for visible-light computing
science.summary: Using a new technique that can create vacancies at any site across a material and then shrink it to about 1/2,000 of its original volume, MIT researchers have designed nanotechnology devices that could be used for optical computing and other applications involving the manipulation of visible light.

science.title: Bright blazar reveals 433-day optical quasi-periodic oscillation across nine years
science.summary: By analyzing the data from the Whole Earth Blazar Telescope (WEBT), an international team of astronomers has discovered optical quasi-periodic oscillation (QPO) in a bright quasar known as 3C 454.3. It is so far one of the most persistent QPOs detected in the optical band. The finding was reported in a paper published April 30 on the arXiv pre-print server.

science.title: Hidden changes in plant reproduction reveal new clues about evolution of self-fertilization
science.summary: In flowering plants, the transition from cross-fertilization (outcrossing) to self-fertilization has evolved repeatedly across species. This shift is often accompanied by a well-known set of traits collectively called the "selfing syndrome," including smaller flowers and reduced scent. However, a new study highlights that important but largely overlooked changes also occur at the microscopic level of pollen biology.

science.title: Atoms vibrate on circular paths—with an unexpected twist
science.summary: An international team of researchers, including scientists from HZDR and Fritz Haber Institute of the Max Planck Society, for the first time directly observed how angular momentum is transferred and conserved within a crystal lattice. Using intense terahertz laser pulses, the researchers were able to selectively control these processes, which unveiled a surprising effect: During the angular momentum transfer, the direction of rotation reverses—caused by the rotational symmetry of the material.

science.title: New Ultra-Black Coating Could Enable the Search for Life on Exoplanets
science.summary: A recently developed ultra-black coating not only efficiently absorbs light, but is also extremely thin and durable, enabling its potential use on starshades that could someday support the imaging of exoplanets and potentially facilitate the detection of life beyond our solar system.

environment.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
environment.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

environment.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
environment.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

environment.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
environment.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

environment.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
environment.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

environment.title: Lake Coatepeque
environment.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

environment.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
environment.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

environment.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
environment.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

environment.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
environment.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

mix_items.title: 😂 Fanii lui Georgescu știu de unde are acesta milioane pentru donații: din salariul de la Real Madrid
mix_items.summary: Simpatizanții lui Călin Georgescu râd de cei care neagă că gurul lor ar fi donat 4 milioane de dolari din banii personali orfanilor din România. Argumentele aduse de aceștia sunt considerate de-a dreptul ridicole. „Cum poți să spui că Domnul Georgescu nu avea acei bani, cât de ticălos să fii să susții așa ceva?”, a […] The post Fanii lui Georgescu știu de unde are acesta milioane pentru donații: din salariul de la Real Madrid appeared first on Times New Roman .

mix_items.title: Agent telemarketing (team leader)
mix_items.summary: Locație: București Contract: Part time (remote) Credem în cauza SOS Satele Copiilor și știm că fiecare copil merită să crească într-o familie iubitoare. De 30 de ani SOS Satele Copiilor... The post Agent telemarketing (team leader) appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: Fost comandant al unităţii de paraşutişti antitero de la SRI, „transferat” de Pro TV. Ce rol va avea
mix_items.summary: Postul de televiziune Pro TV a anunțat că de acum înainte va avea o nouă poziţie: cea de Head of Security, care va fi ocupată de Cătălin Netcu, scrie marți Pagina de Media. Cătălin Netcu a condus, în trecut, o unitate SRI și, în noua poziție, „va coordona strategia de securitate a companiei, cu responsabilităţi …

mix_items.title: Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge
mix_items.summary: Alexandru are 10 ani, locuiește într-un sat din județul Giurgiu și este elev în clasa a III‑a. Este un copil cu... Articolul Visul lui Alexandru începe cu o minge apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025
mix_items.summary: Programul național Telefonul Vârstnicului – 0800 460 001 Sprijinul media a venit de la Spot TV – Pro TV, TVR, Antena 1, Antena 3 CNN, Antena Stars, Prima TV, Prima News, Pro Cinema, Pro Arena, Acasă, Acasă Gold, Trinitas TV, Speranța TV, Antena 3 Deva,... Post-ul SPRIJIN MEDIA OFERIT PROGRAMELOR FUNDAȚIEI REGALE ÎN ANUL 2025 apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București!
mix_items.summary: Cine suntem noi și de cine avem nevoie? Suntem echipa de la Telefonul Vârstnicului, serviciu social licențiat, singura linie telefonică gratuită și confidențială, accesibilă la nivel național, dedicată persoanelor vârstnice, ce oferă consiliere socială și alinarea singurătății. În fiecare an suntem sunați de peste 5,000... Post-ul Angajăm Asistent Social pentru Telefonul Vârstnicului la București! apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: One drug, two cleanup crews: A built-in backup for targeted protein degradation
mix_items.summary: Most drugs work by inhibition: they block a protein's activity but leave the protein itself intact. Targeted protein degradation takes a fundamentally different approach, harnessing the cell's own quality-control machinery to remove proteins entirely.

mix_items.title: Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas
mix_items.summary: Un drum construit prin educație Simona este elevă în clasa a XI-a, la specializarea Tehnician în industria alimentară, la un... Articolul Simona, eleva care descoperă viitorul industriei alimentare pas cu pas apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale
mix_items.summary: București, aprilie 2026 – Într-o lume în care cererea de hrană crește accelerat, iar presiunea asupra resurselor naturale este tot... Articolul Agricultura viitorului: World Vision România sprijină elevii din liceele agricole să devină fermierii erei digitale apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: Tiny worms, with help from researchers, may hold key to treating rare childhood disease
mix_items.summary: A new worm model developed by Brown University researchers could play a key role in treating a rare genetic disease that causes paralysis in children and worsens with age. Developed in the lab of neuroscientist Anne Hart, a genetically engineered C. elegans nematode model provides a fast, inexpensive way to evaluate potential drug treatments for alternating hemiplegia of childhood, or AHC, a disorder that currently has no cure or effective treatments.

mix_items.title: Psiholog
mix_items.summary: Locație: București | Nivel carieră: Experiență de minimum 1 an Candidatul Ideal: Ești tu vocea de care au nevoie? Un copil are nevoie de iubire, siguranță și încredere pentru a... The post Psiholog appeared first on SOS Satele Copiilor România .

mix_items.title: „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase.
mix_items.summary: Într-o casă modestă din județul Vaslui, unde mirosul de lemne arse se amestecă cu zgomotul pașilor mici de copii și... Articolul „Școala e drumul vostru”. Învățăturile unei mame cu 10 clase. apare prima dată în World Vision România .

mix_items.title: We found hundreds of huge ancient mass graves hidden in the Sahara desert
mix_items.summary: We have been on a years-long campaign of satellite remote sensing of the vast desert landscapes in Eastern Sudan.

mix_items.title: De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță
mix_items.summary: În timp ce pentru mulți români Paștele este sărbătoarea revederii și a meselor în familie, pentru mulți vârstnici singuri aceste zile sunt printre cele mai grele din an. Singurătatea doare, lipsurile și sentimentul de abandon se simt mai apăsat tocmai atunci când toți ceilalți par... Post-ul De Paște, 900 de vârstnici singuri primesc prezență umană, ascultare și speranță apare prima dată în Fundația Regală Margareta a României .

mix_items.title: Birds can suffer serious harm from heat waves
mix_items.summary: Extreme weather poses a big threat to birds. Yet there is a lack of both knowledge and methods for measuring its negative effects. In a new study published in Trends in Ecology & Evolution, researchers from Lund University in Sweden have identified knowledge gaps and propose practical tools for analyzing the risks to birds.

mix_items.title: Research into Friedreich's ataxia reveals how DNA folding can silence a key gene
mix_items.summary: Researchers have uncovered a fundamental rule that governs how genes are physically arranged inside the cell nucleus, and how disruptions to that organization can contribute to human disease.

mix_items.title: Scientists use AI to interpret the sun's acoustic heartbeat
mix_items.summary: A new AI-based approach that can "hear" inside the sun could give vital signs of the solar disturbances that have significant effects in near-Earth space and on human activities. The solar cycle is an approximate 11-year period during which the sun's magnetic activity rises and falls. The cycle begins relatively calmly. However, as it progresses, the magnetic field becomes more aggressive, leading to a surge in sunspot numbers, solar flares, and coronal mass ejections. These eruptions can disrupt satellites and power grids and thus have a direct impact on our communication technology.

mix_items.title: AI chatbot shows promise in combating health misinformation
mix_items.summary: Can AI help people resist misinformation? Initial research findings suggest that artificial intelligence-driven conversations can strengthen people's resilience to health misinformation, outperforming traditional educational methods.

mix_items.title: Engineered exosomes reverse sleep deprivation brain damage in mice
mix_items.summary: Sleep is a vital physiological process that allows humans and other animals to restore both the mind and body, while also consolidating memories, clearing out toxins and regulating their metabolism. Several past studies showed that getting insufficient sleep for prolonged periods of time can trigger inflammatory responses and can negatively impact people's memory, mood, attention and decision-making.

mix_items.title: Heat-related hospitalizations rose over two decades. Black adults, low-income communities disproportionately affected
mix_items.summary: A study examined racial and ethnic disparities in rates of heat-related illness (HRI) hospitalizations among U.S. adults from 1998 to 2022. The findings showed that hospitalization rates for HRIs have increased overall in the U.S., with Black adults experiencing the highest rates and greatest increases. People residing in low-income ZIP codes were also disproportionately affected. The findings are published in the Annals of Internal Medicine.

mix_items.title: Hello Universe: NASA’s Next-Gen Space Processor Undergoes Testing
mix_items.summary: NASA’s High Performance Spaceflight Computing project aims to dramatically improve the computing power of spacecraft. Missions need processors that can withstand the harsh space environment, so they use chips developed years ago that are hardy and reliable. But upgraded chips are needed to enable the development of autonomous spacecraft, accelerate the rate of scientific discovery […]

mix_items.title: Food allergy researchers predict oral food challenge tests as an obstacle to future food allergy trials
mix_items.summary: A new international perspective led by UNC School of Medicine researchers highlights that oral food challenges, historically considered essential to food allergy clinical trials, are now limiting who can participate and threatening to slow progress in food allergy research.

mix_items.title: Ivermectin prescriptions more than double after celebrity endorses it as a cancer treatment on a high-profile podcast
mix_items.summary: Prescriptions for the anti-parasite medication ivermectin doubled in the months following an endorsement by a celebrity during a high-profile podcast as an off-label cancer treatment, a UCLA-led study finds.

mix_items.title: New quantum protocol breaks distance and speed barriers in fiber networks
mix_items.summary: Scientists at the University of Science and Technology of China have successfully deployed a multi-mode quantum relay network, achieving matter–matter entanglement over 14.5 kilometers, according to media reports.

mix_items.title: I Am Artemis: Kathleen Harmon
mix_items.summary: Listen to this audio excerpt from Kathleen Harmon, the Artemis II Mission Interface Manager for NASA’s Deep Space Network: Captivated by Apollo launches on her television as a child, Kathleen Harmon now plays a key role in NASA’s Artemis program. Harmon serves as the Artemis II mission interface manager for NASA’s Deep Space Network, an […]

mix_items.title: 'Implosion carving' shrinks 3D photonic devices 2,000-fold for visible-light computing
mix_items.summary: Using a new technique that can create vacancies at any site across a material and then shrink it to about 1/2,000 of its original volume, MIT researchers have designed nanotechnology devices that could be used for optical computing and other applications involving the manipulation of visible light.

mix_items.title: Bright blazar reveals 433-day optical quasi-periodic oscillation across nine years
mix_items.summary: By analyzing the data from the Whole Earth Blazar Telescope (WEBT), an international team of astronomers has discovered optical quasi-periodic oscillation (QPO) in a bright quasar known as 3C 454.3. It is so far one of the most persistent QPOs detected in the optical band. The finding was reported in a paper published April 30 on the arXiv pre-print server.

mix_items.title: Hidden changes in plant reproduction reveal new clues about evolution of self-fertilization
mix_items.summary: In flowering plants, the transition from cross-fertilization (outcrossing) to self-fertilization has evolved repeatedly across species. This shift is often accompanied by a well-known set of traits collectively called the "selfing syndrome," including smaller flowers and reduced scent. However, a new study highlights that important but largely overlooked changes also occur at the microscopic level of pollen biology.

mix_items.title: More than two million Australian middle-aged men are drinking at risky levels
mix_items.summary: The first national in-depth study to examine alcohol use among Australian middle-aged men has found that more than 2 million are drinking at levels that put their health at risk of harm. The new Flinders University research, in partnership with the Alcohol and Drug Foundation (ADF), analyzed national data from the 2019 and 2022/23 National Drug Strategy Household Survey to identify factors linked to risky drinking among midlife men aged 30–59, comparing the younger (30–44) and older (45–59) age groups.

mix_items.title: Study suggests immunotherapy may strengthen treatment for aggressive prostate cancer
mix_items.summary: Researchers at Moffitt Cancer Center report encouraging early results from a phase 2 study examining whether immunotherapy can improve results when added to a radiotherapy-based treatment regimen for men with aggressive prostate cancer. The work, led by Kosj Yamoah, MD, Ph.D., was published in the Journal for ImmunoTherapy of Cancer.

mix_items.title: Are your meds and DNA a bad match? This test alerts clinicians
mix_items.summary: Differences in people's genes can affect how they respond to medications, including how well a drug works and the chances of side effects. About 1 in 10 of prescriptions given to patients outside the hospital don't work as well as they should because of differences in people's genes, according to a review of U.S. studies by UC San Francisco health economics professor Kathryn Phillips, Ph.D., and colleagues.

mix_items.title: Atoms vibrate on circular paths—with an unexpected twist
mix_items.summary: An international team of researchers, including scientists from HZDR and Fritz Haber Institute of the Max Planck Society, for the first time directly observed how angular momentum is transferred and conserved within a crystal lattice. Using intense terahertz laser pulses, the researchers were able to selectively control these processes, which unveiled a surprising effect: During the angular momentum transfer, the direction of rotation reverses—caused by the rotational symmetry of the material.

mix_items.title: A two-way street: The overlapping world of premenstrual disorders and mental health conditions
mix_items.summary: Premenstrual disorders and psychiatric conditions often seem to flock together, and researchers now have data to make a case for it. A large Swedish study involving over 3 million women revealed a striking two-way path between premenstrual disorders (PMDs)—premenstrual dysphoric disorder (PMDD) and premenstrual syndrome (PMS)—and common mental health conditions like depression and anxiety.

mix_items.title: New Ultra-Black Coating Could Enable the Search for Life on Exoplanets
mix_items.summary: A recently developed ultra-black coating not only efficiently absorbs light, but is also extremely thin and durable, enabling its potential use on starshades that could someday support the imaging of exoplanets and potentially facilitate the detection of life beyond our solar system.

mix_items.title: Boosting one protein helps the brain fight Alzheimer’s
mix_items.summary: Scientists have discovered a way to help the brain clean itself of harmful Alzheimer’s plaques by activating its own support cells. By increasing a protein called Sox9, researchers were able to boost the activity of astrocytes, star shaped cells that help maintain brain health. In mice that already showed memory problems, this approach reduced plaque buildup and preserved cognitive function over time.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover a hidden brain “cleaning” effect triggered by movement
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered a surprising link between simple body movement and brain health: every time you tighten your abdominal muscles—even slightly—your brain may gently sway inside your skull. This subtle motion, triggered by pressure changes in connected blood vessels, appears to help circulate cerebrospinal fluid around the brain, potentially flushing out harmful waste.

mix_items.title: Pesticide exposure linked to 150% higher cancer risk in major study
mix_items.summary: A major new study finds that living in pesticide-heavy environments could raise cancer risk by up to 150%, even when the chemicals are considered “safe” on their own. The research suggests these mixtures may silently damage cells years before cancer appears.

mix_items.title: This 2-pound dinosaur is rewriting what scientists know about evolution
mix_items.summary: A nearly complete dinosaur skeleton discovered in Patagonia is helping scientists crack the mystery of alvarezsaurs, a bizarre group of bird-like dinosaurs. The fossil of Alnashetri cerropoliciensis reveals that these animals became tiny before developing their later specialized features, such as stubby arms and ant-eating adaptations. Weighing under two pounds, the dinosaur is one of the smallest known from South America.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally solve the mystery of yeast’s tiny centromeres
mix_items.summary: Scientists have uncovered how brewer’s yeast developed its unusually tiny centromeres, the DNA regions that guide chromosome separation during cell division. By studying related yeast species, researchers found centromeres that appear to represent evolutionary halfway points. These structures seem to have formed from retrotransposons—mobile “jumping genes” in the genome. The discovery shows how DNA once considered genomic junk can be transformed into essential chromosome machinery.

mix_items.title: Light-guided evolution creates proteins that can switch, sense, and compute
mix_items.summary: Researchers have created a method called optovolution that uses light to guide the evolution of proteins with dynamic behaviors. By engineering yeast cells so their survival depended on proteins switching states at the right time, scientists could rapidly select the best-performing variants. The technique produced new light-sensitive proteins that respond to different colors and improved optogenetic systems. It even evolved a protein that behaves like a tiny logic gate, activating genes only when two signals are present.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover hidden species among Borneo’s “fanged frogs”
mix_items.summary: DNA is revealing that many animals once thought to be a single species may actually be several hidden ones. But research on Bornean fanged frogs shows the line between species can be blurry—an important challenge when deciding what wildlife needs protection most.

mix_items.title: Lake Coatepeque
mix_items.summary: Set amid El Salvador’s modern, active volcanic landscape, tranquil blue waters fill a caldera formed by ancient eruptions. The post Lake Coatepeque appeared first on NASA Science .

mix_items.title: Scientists stunned to find signs of ancient life in a place no one expected
mix_items.summary: While exploring ancient seabeds in Morocco, scientists discovered strange wrinkle-like textures in deep-water sediments that shouldn’t have been there. These structures are usually made by sunlight-loving microbial mats in shallow waters. But the rocks formed far below the reach of light, suggesting a different explanation. Evidence points to chemosynthetic microbes—organisms powered by chemical reactions—creating the mats in the dark depths of an ancient ocean.

mix_items.title: Scientists finally reveal why mint feels cold
mix_items.summary: Scientists have revealed how the body’s microscopic cold sensor, TRPM8, detects both chilly temperatures and the cooling effect of menthol. The discovery finally shows how the sensation of “cool” works at the molecular level—and could inspire new treatments for pain and eye disorders.

mix_items.title: Bird droppings helped build one of ancient Peru’s most powerful kingdoms
mix_items.summary: New research suggests seabird guano helped transform the Chincha Kingdom into one of the most prosperous societies in ancient Peru. Chemical clues in centuries-old maize show farmers fertilized their crops with guano gathered from nearby islands, dramatically boosting yields in the desert landscape. The resulting agricultural surplus fueled trade, population growth, and regional influence.

joke_ro.text: Azi n-am procrastinat deloc. Am amanat direct toata ziua.

satire_ro.title: Marius Bucă și Ion Criscoiu, campioni la umor după ce i-au zis lui Bolojan „Bolovan”
